safety_flags.label: Relying heavily on substances to sleep
Sleep problems severely impair daily functioning
Severe hopelessness — cannot imagine improvement
Strong sense of being a burden to others
Passive suicidal ideation reported
Active self-harm or suicidal thoughts reported
Frequent and persistent suicidal ideation
User may have a plan for self-harm
User has ideation but has not sought help
Frequent, severe panic attacks reported
Possible disordered eating pattern
Significant self-neglect of physical health

responses.question_text: I have been having trouble sleeping.
I struggle to fall asleep when I first get into bed.
Thoughts race through my mind and prevent me from falling asleep.
I feel anxious or tense when I get into bed at night.
I wake up multiple times during the night.
I wake up much earlier than I intend to and cannot fall back asleep.
I have disturbing dreams or nightmares that interrupt my sleep.
Physical discomfort such as pain or needing to use the bathroom wakes me up.
I feel unrefreshed and tired even after a full night's sleep.
I experience significant daytime sleepiness that affects my functioning.
Poor sleep noticeably worsens my mood, patience, or ability to concentrate.
My sleep and wake times vary greatly from day to day.
I often stay up much later than I intend to.
I use screens or devices in bed immediately before trying to sleep.
I use alcohol, medication, or other substances to help me fall asleep.
My sleep problems have significantly disrupted my work, relationships, or quality of life.
I have been feeling persistently sad, empty, or low.
My low mood is present for most of the day, nearly every day.
I feel tearful or emotionally numb without a clear external reason.
My low mood has persisted for several weeks rather than just a few days.
I have lost interest in hobbies or activities I used to enjoy.
I no longer look forward to things the way I once did.
Even when I do things I used to enjoy, I feel little or no pleasure.
I have withdrawn from social activities because they feel meaningless or joyless.
I feel hopeless about the future.
I find it very hard to imagine things getting better.
I feel like a burden to the people around me.
I have given up on goals or plans that once mattered to me.
I feel worthless or like I am a failure.
I blame myself excessively for things that go wrong.
I feel deep shame about who I am or things I have done.
My sense of self-worth depends heavily on the approval of others.
I have been excessively self-critical in ways that feel difficult to control.
I have had thoughts that life is not worth living.
I have had thoughts about harming myself or ending my life.
These thoughts have been frequent or very difficult to dismiss.
I have made or thought about making a specific plan related to self-harm.
I have spoken to a friend, family member, or professional about these thoughts.
I feel tense, on edge, or keyed up.
I find it hard to relax even when there is no immediate pressure.
I feel restless and have difficulty sitting still.
I am easily startled or feel unusually jumpy.
My anxiety feels constant and very difficult to switch off.
I worry excessively about multiple areas of my life at once.
My worries feel out of proportion to the actual situation.
I find it very difficult to stop or control my worrying.
I automatically jump to imagining worst-case scenarios.
Worrying takes up a significant amount of my time and mental energy each day.
I experience physical symptoms of anxiety such as a racing heart or shortness of breath.
I have had sudden surges of intense, overwhelming fear.
During anxious moments I feel dizzy, nauseous, or physically unwell.
I have experienced panic attacks — sudden overwhelming fear with strong physical symptoms.
My physical anxiety symptoms interfere with my ability to function normally.
I avoid situations, places, or activities because they make me anxious.
My avoidance has caused me to miss out on important events or opportunities.
I need reassurance from others to feel comfortable making everyday decisions.
I have developed repetitive rituals or safety behaviors to manage my anxiety.
My anxiety significantly restricts my life or prevents me from doing things I want to do.
I feel genuinely connected to and supported by the people in my life.
I feel physically or mentally exhausted for most of the day.
My energy levels are significantly lower than they used to be.
I feel fatigued even after minimal physical or mental effort.
My fatigue makes it difficult to complete everyday tasks.
I feel physically heavy or as though I am moving through mud.
I have stopped or significantly reduced physical activity because I lack the energy.
My fatigue has no clear physical explanation such as illness or medication.
My appetite has noticeably increased or decreased recently.
I eat significantly more or less than I used to.
My relationship with food feels out of control or is causing me distress.
I have noticed significant or unwanted changes in my body weight recently.
I tend to skip meals or overeat in response to stress or emotional states.
I experience physical symptoms such as headaches, stomach problems, or body pain when under stress.
I neglect my physical health — diet, exercise, or medical care — because of how I feel emotionally.
I find it very difficult to concentrate on tasks.
My mind wanders frequently, even during important conversations or activities.
I frequently have to re-read things or redo tasks because I could not focus the first time.
I struggle to stay mentally present during meetings, classes, or conversations.
My ability to concentrate feels noticeably worse than it used to be.
Poor concentration has negatively affected my work, studies, or important responsibilities.
I find it hard to make decisions, even relatively minor ones.
I frequently second-guess decisions I have already made.
My memory feels noticeably worse than it used to be.
I forget things I would previously have remembered easily.
My thinking feels slow, foggy, or unclear — sometimes called brain fog.
Difficulties with thinking, memory, or decisions have significantly impacted my daily life.

During anxious moments I feel dizzy, nauseous, or physically unwell.
I have experienced panic attacks — sudden overwhelming fear with strong physical symptoms.
My physical anxiety symptoms interfere with my ability to function normally.
I avoid situations, places, or activities because they make me anxious.
My avoidance has caused me to miss out on important events or opportunities.
I need reassurance from others to feel comfortable making everyday decisions.
I have developed repetitive rituals or safety behaviors to manage my anxiety.
My anxiety significantly restricts my life or prevents me from doing things I want to do.
I feel genuinely connected to and supported by the people in my life.
I feel physically or mentally exhausted for most of the day.
My energy levels are significantly lower than they used to be.
I feel fatigued even after minimal physical or mental effort.
My fatigue makes it difficult to complete everyday tasks.
I feel physically heavy or as though I am moving through mud.
I have stopped or significantly reduced physical activity because I lack the energy.
My fatigue has no clear physical explanation such as illness or medication.
My appetite has noticeably increased or decreased recently.
I eat significantly more or less than I used to.
My relationship with food feels out of control or is causing me distress.
I have noticed significant or unwanted changes in my body weight recently.
I tend to skip meals or overeat in response to stress or emotional states.
I experience physical symptoms such as headaches, stomach problems, or body pain when under stress.
I neglect my physical health — diet, exercise, or medical care — because of how I feel emotionally.
I find it very difficult to concentrate on tasks.
My mind wanders frequently, even during important conversations or activities.
I frequently have to re-read things or redo tasks because I could not focus the first time.
I struggle to stay mentally present during meetings, classes, or conversations.
My ability to concentrate feels noticeably worse than it used to be.
Poor concentration has negatively affected my work, studies, or important responsibilities.
I find it hard to make decisions, even relatively minor ones.
I frequently second-guess decisions I have already made.
My memory feels noticeably worse than it used to be.
I forget things I would previously have remembered easily.
My thinking feels slow, foggy, or unclear — sometimes called brain fog.
Difficulties with thinking, memory, or decisions have significantly impacted my daily life.
